# lenticular-fx

> A React component that recreates the lenticular print effect — sliding the cursor over a wrapper restripes between two stacked `front` and `back` children through interleaved vertical columns, exactly like a physical lenticular sticker, trading card, or postcard. Pure CSS `clip-path` + `requestAnimationFrame`, zero runtime dependencies, ~10 kB packed.

## Install

```bash
bun add lenticular-fx
# or
npm install lenticular-fx
# or
pnpm add lenticular-fx
```

## API

```tsx
import { Lenticular } from 'lenticular-fx'

<Lenticular
  front={<Day />}
  back={<Night />}
  slices={12}      // 2–40 interleaved columns
  ease={0.12}      // lerp per frame, 0.01–1
  tilt={14}        // optional 3D rotateY degrees
  hitMargin={24}   // extra interactive padding in px
  defaultSide="front"
/>
```

## Props (full)

- `front: ReactNode` — content on the left edge (offset 0). Required.
- `back: ReactNode` — content on the right edge (offset 1). Required.
- `slices?: number` — number of interleaved vertical columns. Clamped [2, 40]. Default 10.
- `ease?: number` — lerp factor per frame. 1 = instant, 0.01 = molasses. Clamped (0, 1]. Default 0.12.
- `defaultSide?: 'front' | 'back'` — face at rest. Default 'front'.
- `trackWindow?: boolean` — track cursor across the whole viewport. Default false.
- `hitMargin?: number` — extra hit-area in px so the cursor can overshoot the visible edge and still hold the back fully revealed. Default 24.
- `tilt?: number` — optional 3D rotateY in degrees, driven by the same offset. Default 0 (off).
- `perspective?: number` — CSS perspective in px when `tilt > 0`. Default 900.
- `gyroscope?: boolean` — use device-orientation gamma instead of mouse on supported devices. Default false.
- `paused?: boolean` — freeze the animation loop. Default false.
- `onOffsetChange?: (offset: number) => void` — called each frame with the lerped offset. Use a ref, not setState.
- `className?: string` — applied to the wrapper div.
- `style?: CSSProperties` — applied to the wrapper div.

All `HTMLDivElement` attributes are forwarded to the wrapper. The component forwards a ref to the wrapper div.

## How it works

A physical lenticular print is alternating columns of two images placed behind a ribbed lens. We simulate that with two stacked overlay layers above an invisible layout base, each cut to vertical rectangles by `clip-path: path()`. On `mousemove`, the cursor X is normalized to [0, 1] relative to the wrapper. A `requestAnimationFrame` loop lerps a `currentOffset` ref toward that target — React never re-renders inside the animation. Each frame, two clip-path strings are recomputed: the front layer's rectangle spans the left `(1 − offset)` of every slice, the back layer's the remaining right portion. Sub-pixel cutoffs collapse to a single rectangle or hide entirely so there are no anti-aliasing seams at the extremes.

## Browser support

- Chrome / Edge 88+
- Safari 14.1+
- Firefox 63+

## Use cases

- Product cards that flip to spec sheets on hover
- Avatar / portrait day → night reveals
- CTA buttons that swap copy ("Get started" → "14-day free trial")
- Trading cards with holographic-feeling interleave
- Tickers and marquees that morph between two phrases
- Anything you'd hand-print on lenticular plastic
